section: Behavior of Fe-Mn-Al-C Steels during Cyclic Tests

context_update.summary: This chunk introduces the cyclic behavior of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel. It states that the alloy has higher fatigue strength at 523–823 K than at room temperature, with a maximum at 723 K for 2×10^7 cycles. As-quenched Fe-29Mn-9Al-0.9C outperforms Cr-Ni steels but is inferior to martensitic Cr steels when no plastic deformation occurs. Experimental details: ESR ingot composition Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.86C-0.7W-0.43Mo-0.49Nb, water quenched from 1323 K, rotating bending fatigue baseline 2×10^7 cycles, thermal cycling (873–313 K, 80% restriction), and elasto-plastic bending at constant 150 mm deflection. Fatigue tests at 673 K show strengthening but not to the maximum level; thermocycling (673 K heat / 293 K pulse cooling) yields fatigue curves between the 293 K and 673 K curves, indicating time-dependent deformation aging. Thermal fatigue life: Fe-Mn-Al-C steel endures 16,000 cycles, nearly twice the 9,000 cycles of the martensitic chromium steel. Microstructure after thermal cycling: thickening of grain boundary κ precipitates in Fe-Mn-Al-C, while the martensitic steel decomposes into ferrite-carbide mixture with loss of martensitic orientation and strength. Figures: Fig. 1 (test setups), Fig. 2 (fatigue curves), Fig. 3 (micrographs). These processing–structure–property links are essential for subsequent analysis of elasto-plastic deformation and thermal fatigue mechanisms.
key_variables: temperature (293 K, 673 K, 723 K, 873 K, 313 K)
specimen diameters (5 mm, 20 mm, 50 mm, 60 mm)
properties: thermal fatigue life 16,000 cycles vs 9,000 cycles
mechanisms: deformation aging in microplastic zones at higher temperatures

context_update.summary: Chunk 2 completes Section 3.2 Thermal Fatigue Tests (only microstructural evolution of martensitic steel after thermal fatigue) and covers Section 3.3 Cyclic Tests in Elasto-Plastic Deformation, along with the Conclusions. For the martensitic chromium steel, thermal cycling leads to decomposition into a ferrite-carbide mixture with loss of martensitic orientation and decreased strength (Fig. 3c,d). Elasto-plastic bending tests show that the hysteresis loop energy remains practically invariant cycle to cycle (Fig. 4), and the Fe-Mn-Al-C steel endures four times more cycles than Fe-13Cr-0.2C martensitic steel. Fatigue S-N data are tabulated for 293 K, 673 K, and thermocycling 293 K↔673 K (Fig. 2), providing specific stress values at 0.1–50 million cycles. The high strengthening capacity is linked to mechanical twinning as the primary deformation mechanism, with twins acting as obstacles to dislocation slip and twinning (Fig. 5). Strain hardening curve data (true stress vs. true strain at 1 mm/min) are given. TEM observations (Fig. 6) show a sharp increase in dislocation density and localized dislocation tangles at 10% and 20% cold work. A cyclic strengthening model is proposed: each cycle creates highly strengthened local volumes, continuously forming new sites, which explains high endurance and constant energy absorption; failure occurs when the entire volume has been processed. Conclusions state that Fe-Mn-Al-C steel is suitable for elastoplastic cyclic service, periodic temperature changes are not detrimental, and fatigue strength depends on temperature and exposure time. Subsequent chunks should carry forward the S-N data, twinning/dislocation mechanisms, strain hardening curve, and the comparative bending endurance.
key_variables: fatigue test temperatures: 293 K, 673 K, 293 K ↔ 673 K
microstructure: ferrite-carbide mixture in martensitic steel after thermal fatigue
properties: invariant hysteresis loop energy in bending
fatigue stress values at various N (Fig. 2)
4× more cycles to failure in bending vs. Fe-13Cr-0.2C
mechanisms: mechanical twinning as primary deformation mechanism
twins as obstacles to slip and twinning
cyclic creation of localized strengthened volumes
cumulative processing of entire volume leading to failure
figures_or_tables: Figure 2 (fatigue S-N curves with data points)
Figure 6 (TEM images of dislocations at 10% and 20%)

Elasto-plastic bending tests show that the hysteresis loop energy remains practically invariant cycle to cycle (Fig. 4), and the Fe-Mn-Al-C steel endures four times more cycles than Fe-13Cr-0.2C martensitic steel. The high strengthening capacity is linked to mechanical twinning as the primary deformation mechanism, with twins acting as obstacles to dislocation slip and twinning (Fig. 5). Strain hardening curve data (true stress vs. true strain at 1 mm/min) are given. TEM observations (Fig. 6) show a sharp increase in dislocation density and localized dislocation tangles at 10% and 20% cold work. A cyclic strengthening model is proposed: each cycle creates highly strengthened local volumes, continuously forming new sites, which explains high endurance and constant energy absorption; failure occurs when the entire volume has been processed. Conclusions state that Fe-Mn-Al-C steel is suitable for elastoplastic cyclic service, periodic temperature changes are not detrimental, and fatigue strength depends on temperature and exposure time.
